result.markdown:
# Forecasting with `fpp3` ::: {.cell} ::: ## Introduction. {.unnumbered} Forecast methods are essential in business and finance for strategic decision-making, efficient resource allocation, and financial planning. Accurate forecasts enable organizations to identify and manage risks, attract investor confidence, stay competitive in the market, optimize supply chains, and evaluate performance against benchmarks. By anticipating changes in the business environment, businesses can adapt proactively and comply with regulatory requirements. Overall, forecasting provides a structured approach to planning, allowing businesses to navigate uncertainties and achieve long-term success. ## The forecast problem. The problem is to forecast a time series. In particular, the time series is the *Beer, Wine, and Distilled Alcoholic Beverages Sales* as in the original Matt Dancho's example. The data is taken from FRED (Federal Reserve Economic Data). The data belongs to the non-durable goods category, it includes U.S. merchant wholesalers, except manufacturers' sales branches and offices sales. The monthly time series goes from 2010-01-01 to 2022-10-31. And the goal is to use 2022 data (10 months) as a test data to conduct the forecast. For the full database details see: https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/S4248SM144NCEN Before start, see: What is forecasting? ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} embed_url("https://youtu.be/zvd9oboayEc?si=DfK9JkSqapE8JHiw") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### The data. Let's load the R packages. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Load libraries library(fpp3) library(timetk) # Toolkit for working with time series in R. library(tidyquant) # Loads tidyverse, financial pkgs, used to get data. library(dplyr) # Database manipulation. library(ggplot2) # Plots. library(tibble) # Tables. library(kableExtra) # Tables. library(knitr) library(bit64) # Useful in the machine learning workflow. library(sweep) # Broom-style tidiers for the forecast package. library(forecast) # Forecasting models and predictions package. library(seasonal) library(tictoc) ``` ::: We can conveniently download the data directly from the FRED API in one line of code. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Beer, Wine, Distilled Alcoholic Beverages, in Millions USD. beer <- tq_get("S4248SM144NCEN", get = "economic.data", from = "2010-01-01", to = "2022-10-31") ``` ::: Let's have a look of the data set. By default it says *price*, but these are sales figures in monetary terms. According to the main FRED reference, these are in millions of dollars, not seasonally adjusted. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} head(beer) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 3 symbol date price 1 S4248SM144NCEN 2010-01-01 6558 2 S4248SM144NCEN 2010-02-01 7481 3 S4248SM144NCEN 2010-03-01 9475 4 S4248SM144NCEN 2010-04-01 9424 5 S4248SM144NCEN 2010-05-01 9351 6 S4248SM144NCEN 2010-06-01 10552 ``` ::: ::: We can change the name of the price column. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer <- beer %>% rename(sales = price) tail(beer) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 3 symbol date sales 1 S4248SM144NCEN 2022-05-01 16714 2 S4248SM144NCEN 2022-06-01 17899 3 S4248SM144NCEN 2022-07-01 15193 4 S4248SM144NCEN 2022-08-01 17069 5 S4248SM144NCEN 2022-09-01 16443 6 S4248SM144NCEN 2022-10-01 15585 ``` ::: ::: Better now. Visualization is particularly important for time series analysis and forecasting. It’s a good idea to identify spots where we will split the data into training and test. This kind of split is consistent with most machine learning algorithms. The *training* dataset is the sample of data used to fit and train the model by learning from the data. The *test* dataset is the sample of data used to provide an unbiased evaluation of a final model fit on the training dataset. The test dataset provides the gold standard used to evaluate the model. It is only used once a model is completely trained. The test set is generally what is used to evaluate competing models. It is also important to see the time series because normally the models will perform better if we can identify basic characteristics such as trend and seasonality. This data set clearly has a trend and a seasonality as people drink more alcohol in December. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer %>% ggplot(aes(date, sales)) + # Train Region: annotate("text", x = ymd("2013-01-01"), y = 14000, color = "black", label = "Train region") + geom_rect(xmin = as.numeric(ymd("2022-01-01")), xmax = as.numeric(ymd("2022-09-30")), ymin = 0, ymax = 20000, alpha = 0.02, fill = "pink") + annotate("text", x = ymd("2022-06-01"), y = 9000, color = "black", label = "Test\nregion") + # Data. geom_line(col = "black") + geom_point(col = "black", alpha = 0.5, size = 2) + # Aesthetics. theme_tq() + scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") + labs(subtitle = "Train (2010 - 2021), and test set (Jan 2022 to Oct 2022)", x = "Date", y = "Sales", caption = "The models do not know the test region, this is for us to see how well the models do the 10-month ahead forecast.") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Beer, Wine, and Distilled Alcoholic Beverages Sales.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-bwadabs-1.png){#fig-bwadabs width=672} ::: ::: Then, the problem is to forecast the 10 months of the test region. This is, from January to October 2022. Here is a zoom version of the plot above. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer %>% filter(date > as.Date("2020-01-01")) %>% ggplot(aes(date, sales)) + annotate("text", x = ymd("2020-08-01"), y = 17000, color = "black", label = "Train region") + geom_rect(xmin = as.numeric(ymd("2022-01-01")), xmax = as.numeric(ymd("2022-09-30")), ymin = 0, ymax = 20000, alpha = 0.02, fill = "pink") + annotate("text", x = ymd("2022-05-01"), y = 14000, color = "black", label = "Test region") + geom_line(col = "black") + geom_point(col = "black", alpha = 0.5, size = 5) + theme_tq() + scale_x_date(date_breaks = "1 year", date_labels = "%Y") + labs(subtitle = "Train (2010 - 2021) and test set (Jan 2022 to Oct 2022)", x = "Date", y = "Sales", caption = "The models do not know the test region, this is for us to see how well the models do the 10-month ahead forecast.") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom: Beer, Wine, and Distilled Alcoholic Beverages Sales.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-zbwadabs-1.png){#fig-zbwadabs width=672} ::: ::: ### Time series properties. The forecasting techniques are expected to exploit the time-series components like trend and seasonal component. Here we use the @hyndman2021forecasting `fpp3` package to learn about the time series properties before conducting the forecast techniques. In order to use the `fpp3` package, we have to transform `beer` from a `tibble` to a `tsibble` object. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls <- beer beer_tbls$date <- yearmonth(beer_tbls$date) beer_tbls <- as_tsibble(beer_tbls) ``` ::: According to @hyndman2021forecasting, the X-11 method was originated in the US Census Bureau and further developed by Statistics Canada. The decomposition process tends to be highly robust to outliers and level shifts in the time series. The details of the X-11 method are described in @dagum2016seasonal. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% model(x11 = X_13ARIMA_SEATS(sales ~ x11())) %>% components() %>% autoplot() + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![A multiplicative decomposition of beer sales using X-11.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-amdobsux-1.png){#fig-amdobsux width=672} ::: ::: The trend shows a clear change in the first half of 2020. Let's take a look of it. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% model(x11 = X_13ARIMA_SEATS(sales ~ x11())) %>% components() %>% select(date, sales, trend) %>% filter_index("2020-03" ~ "2020-10") %>% head(8) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tsibble: 8 x 3 [1M] date sales trend 1 2020 mar. 13281 13618. 2 2020 abr. 12225 13724. 3 2020 may. 13977 13828. 4 2020 jun. 16065 13914. 5 2020 jul. 15577 15298. 6 2020 ago. 15225 15329. 7 2020 sep. 15446 15338. 8 2020 oct. 16024 15324. ``` ::: ::: The consumption trend significantly increased from June 2020 to July 2020. It is big enough to create a discontinuity in the trend plot below. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% model(x11 = X_13ARIMA_SEATS(sales ~ x11())) %>% components() %>% select(date, trend) %>% ggplot(aes(yearmonth(date), trend)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 4) + geom_point(aes(x = yearmonth("2020 jun."), y = 13950.99), alpha = 0.3, size = 4, colour = "red") + geom_point(aes(x = yearmonth("2020 jul."), y = 15356.13), alpha = 0.3, size = 4, colour = "red") + labs(y = "Trend", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Trend discontinuity.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-td-1.png){#fig-td width=672} ::: ::: There are also three negative spikes in the irregular component. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% model(x11 = X_13ARIMA_SEATS(sales ~ x11())) %>% components() %>% select(date, sales, irregular) %>% filter(irregular < 0.95) %>% head(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tsibble: 3 x 3 [1M] date sales irregular 1 2020 abr. 12225 0.892 2 2020 dic. 16353 0.868 3 2021 dic. 18201 0.912 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% model(x11 = X_13ARIMA_SEATS(sales ~ x11())) %>% components() %>% select(date, irregular) %>% ggplot(aes(yearmonth(date), irregular)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.3, size = 4) + geom_point(aes(x = yearmonth("2020 apr."), y = 0.8894670), alpha = 0.3, size = 4, colour = "red") + geom_point(aes(x = yearmonth("2020 dec."), y = 0.8688517), alpha = 0.3, size = 4, colour = "red") + geom_point(aes(x = yearmonth("2021 dec."), y = 0.9099203), alpha = 0.3, size = 4, colour = "red") + labs(y = "Irregular", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Three negative spikes in the irregular component.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-tnsitic-1.png){#fig-tnsitic width=672} ::: ::: Apparently, these trend and irregular events are consistent independently of the decomposition technique. We implement the SEATS decomposition below. SEATS stands for *Seasonal Extraction in ARIMA Time Series*. According to @hyndman2021forecasting, this procedure was developed at the Bank of Spain, and is now widely used by government agencies around the world. See @dagum2016seasonal for further details. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% model(seats = X_13ARIMA_SEATS(sales ~ seats())) %>% components() %>% autoplot() + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![A decomposition of beer sales obtained using SEATS.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-adobsous-1.png){#fig-adobsous width=672} ::: ::: A seasonal plot is similar to a time plot except that the data are plotted against the individual *seasons*, in this case months, in which the data were observed. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% gg_season(sales, labels = "both") + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Seasonal plot: Beer sales.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-spbs-1.png){#fig-spbs width=672} ::: ::: And this is the zoom version of the plot above. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% filter_index("2019-01" ~ .) %>% gg_season(sales, labels = "both", size = 2) + geom_point() + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom. Seasonal plot: Beer sales.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-zspbs-1.png){#fig-zspbs width=672} ::: ::: An alternative plot that emphasises the seasonal patterns is where the data for each month are collected together in separate mini time plots. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% gg_subseries(sales) + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Seasonal subseries plot of monthly beer sales.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-sspombs-1.png){#fig-sspombs width=672} ::: ::: December is the month of the year with the highest average sales, followed by June. January is the month of the year with the lowest average sales, followed by February. Following @hyndman2021forecasting, just as correlation measures the extent of a linear relationship between two variables, autocorrelation measures the linear relationship between lagged values of a time series. When data have a trend, the autocorrelations for small lags tend to be large and positive because observations nearby in time are also nearby in value. So the ACF of a trended time series tends to have positive values that slowly decrease as the lags increase. When data are seasonal, the autocorrelations will be larger for the seasonal lags (at multiples of the seasonal period, in this case 12) than for other lags. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_tbls %>% ACF(sales, lag_max = 60) %>% autoplot() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![ACF of monthly beer sales.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-aombs-1.png){#fig-aombs width=672} ::: ::: Beer sales is both trended and seasonal. The slow decrease in the ACF as the lags increase is due to the trend, while the *scalloped* shape is due to the seasonality. ## Forecasts with `fpp3`. Here we implement some selected forecast techniques using the @hyndman2021forecasting `fpp3` package. First define the training and test set (2022). ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_train <- beer_tbls %>% select(date, sales) %>% filter_index(. ~ "2021-12") beer_2022 <- beer_tbls %>% select(date, sales) %>% filter_index("2022-01" ~ .) ``` ::: ### Four simple techniques. Let's estimate four simple forecast techniques. *Mean*, where the forecasts of all future values are equal to the average of the historical data. *Naïve*, we set all forecasts to be the value of the last observation. *Seasonal naïve*, we set each forecast to be equal to the last observed value from the same season. And *drift*, to allow the forecasts to increase or decrease over time. Estimate the four models. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_fit <- beer_train %>% model("fpp3: mean" = MEAN(sales), "fpp3: naïve" = NAIVE(sales), "fpp3: seasonal naïve" = SNAIVE(sales), "fpp3: drift" = RW(sales ~ drift())) glance(beer_fit) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 2 .model sigma2 1 fpp3: mean 5430658. 2 fpp3: naïve 3279288. 3 fpp3: seasonal naïve 491364. 4 fpp3: drift 3279288. ``` ::: ::: The 10-month forecasts. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_fc <- beer_fit %>% fabletools::forecast(h = "10 months") ``` ::: Let's compute the MAPE of all forecasts. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mape_table <- fabletools::accuracy(beer_fc, beer_2022) %>% select(.model, MAPE) %>% arrange(desc(-MAPE)) mape_table ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 2 .model MAPE 1 fpp3: seasonal naïve 3.89 2 fpp3: naïve 18.1 3 fpp3: drift 20.9 4 fpp3: mean 23.5 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} sn_mape <- fabletools::accuracy(beer_fc, beer_2022) %>% filter(.model == "fpp3: seasonal naïve") %>% select(MAPE) %>% unlist() sn_mape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` MAPE 3.889793 ``` ::: ::: Let's plot the forecast results. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_fc %>% autoplot(beer_tbls, level = NULL) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2022-01-01"), lty = 2) + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + guides(colour = guide_legend(title = "Forecast:")) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![fpp3: four simple forecasts.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-ffsf-1.png){#fig-ffsf width=672} ::: ::: The plot above is not very clear. Here is a zoom version. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_zoom <- beer_tbls %>% select(date, sales) %>% filter_index("2019-12" ~ .) beer_fc %>% autoplot(beer_zoom, level = NULL, lwd = 2) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2022-01-01"), lty = 2) + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + guides(colour = guide_legend(title = "Forecast:")) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom. fpp3: four simple forecasts.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-zffsf-1.png){#fig-zffsf width=672} ::: ::: This is the seasonal naïve forecasts. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_zoom <- beer_tbls %>% select(date, sales) %>% filter_index("2019-12" ~ .) beer_sn_fc <- beer_fc %>% filter(.model == "fpp3: seasonal naïve") ggplot(beer_zoom, aes(yearmonth(date), sales), lwd = 2, alpha = 0.4) + geom_line() + geom_point(size = 5, color = "black", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "black") + geom_point(aes(y = .mean), size = 5, color = "red", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "red", data = beer_sn_fc) + geom_line(aes(y = .mean), color = "red", size = 0.5, data = beer_sn_fc) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2021-12-01")), linetype = 2) + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date", caption = c(paste("MAPE=",(round(sn_mape, 5))))) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom. fpp3: seasonal naïve forecasts.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-zfsnf-1.png){#fig-zfsnf width=672} ::: ::: The simple techniques are not necessarily bad techniques. ### Exponential smoothing. According to @hyndman2021forecasting, forecasts produced using exponential smoothing methods are weighted averages of past observations, with the weights decaying exponentially as the observations get older. In other words, the more recent the observation the higher the associated weight. This framework generates reliable forecasts quickly and for a wide range of time series, which is a great advantage and of major importance to applications in industry. In this subsection, we let the `ETS()` function select the model by minimising the AICc. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_ets <- beer_train %>% model(ETS(sales)) report(beer_ets) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Series: sales Model: ETS(M,A,M) Smoothing parameters: alpha = 0.1602027 beta = 0.00961933 gamma = 0.0001010184 Initial states: l[0] b[0] s[0] s[-1] s[-2] s[-3] s[-4] s[-5] 9247.67 37.19533 1.168876 1.031489 1.036782 0.993476 1.051993 0.9885615 s[-6] s[-7] s[-8] s[-9] s[-10] s[-11] 1.125236 1.062294 0.9661073 0.9855889 0.8314213 0.7581742 sigma^2: 0.0021 AIC AICc BIC 2536.576 2541.433 2587.063 ``` ::: ::: The `ETS(M,A,M)` corresponds to a Holt-Winters multiplicative method with multiplicative errors for when seasonal variations are changing proportional to the level of the series. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} components(beer_ets) %>% autoplot() + labs(x = "Date", y = "Sales") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![fpp3: ETS components.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-fec-1.png){#fig-fec width=672} ::: ::: The ETS(M,A,M) 10-month forecast. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_ets_fc <- beer_ets %>% fabletools::forecast(h = 10) beer_ets_fc ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A fable: 10 x 4 [1M] # Key: .model [1] .model date 1 ETS(sales) 2022 ene. 2 ETS(sales) 2022 feb. 3 ETS(sales) 2022 mar. 4 ETS(sales) 2022 abr. 5 ETS(sales) 2022 may. 6 ETS(sales) 2022 jun. 7 ETS(sales) 2022 jul. 8 ETS(sales) 2022 ago. 9 ETS(sales) 2022 sep. 10 ETS(sales) 2022 oct. # ℹ 2 more variables: sales , .mean ``` ::: ::: The ETS(M,A,M) MAPE. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ets_mape <- fabletools::accuracy(beer_ets_fc, beer_2022) %>% select(MAPE) %>% unlist() ets_mape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` MAPE 3.7717 ``` ::: ::: Let's see the `ETS(M,A,M)` forecast. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_ets %>% fabletools::forecast(h = 10) %>% autoplot(beer_tbls) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2022-01-01"), lty = 2) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Sales") + theme(legend.position = "bottom") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![fpp3: ETS(M,A,M) forecast.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-fef-1.png){#fig-fef width=672} ::: ::: This is not very clear, here is the zoom version. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(beer_zoom, aes(yearmonth(date), sales), lwd = 2, alpha = 0.4) + geom_line() + geom_point(size = 5, color = "black", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "black") + geom_point(aes(y = .mean), size = 5, color = "red", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "red", data = beer_ets_fc) + geom_line(aes(y = .mean), color = "red", size = 0.5, data = beer_ets_fc) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2021-12-01")), linetype = 2) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Sales", caption = c(paste("MAPE=",(round(ets_mape, 5))))) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom. fpp3: ETS(M,A,M) forecast.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-zfef-1.png){#fig-zfef width=672} ::: ::: Update the MAPE table. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mape_updated <- mape_table %>% add_row(.model = "fpp3: ETS(M,A,M)", MAPE = ets_mape) %>% arrange(desc(-MAPE)) mape_updated ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 5 × 2 .model MAPE 1 fpp3: ETS(M,A,M) 3.77 2 fpp3: seasonal naïve 3.89 3 fpp3: naïve 18.1 4 fpp3: drift 20.9 5 fpp3: mean 23.5 ``` ::: ::: ### ARIMA. According to @hyndman2021forecasting, while exponential smoothing models are based on a description of the trend and seasonality in the data, ARIMA models aim to describe the autocorrelations in the data. The `ARIMA()` function combines unit root tests, minimisation of the AICc and MLE to obtain an ARIMA model. By setting `stepwise = FALSE` and `approximation = FALSE`, we are making R work extra hard to find a good model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_arima <- beer_train %>% model(arima_auto = ARIMA(sales, stepwise = FALSE, approx = FALSE)) report(beer_arima) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Series: sales Model: ARIMA(4,1,1)(0,1,1)[12] Coefficients: ar1 ar2 ar3 ar4 ma1 sma1 -0.0716 -0.0164 0.2239 -0.3366 -0.7664 -0.6620 s.e. 0.1064 0.1049 0.0962 0.0842 0.0830 0.1049 sigma^2 estimated as 311616: log likelihood=-1015.88 AIC=2045.75 AICc=2046.66 BIC=2065.88 ``` ::: ::: The residuals for the best ARIMA model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_arima %>% select(arima_auto) %>% gg_tsresiduals() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Residuals from the fitted fpp3 ARIMA(4,1,1)(0,1,1)[12] model.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-rftffam-1.png){#fig-rftffam width=672} ::: ::: The forecast for the best ARIMA model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_arima_fc <- beer_arima %>% fabletools::forecast(h = "10 months") beer_arima_fc ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A fable: 10 x 4 [1M] # Key: .model [1] .model date 1 arima_auto 2022 ene. 2 arima_auto 2022 feb. 3 arima_auto 2022 mar. 4 arima_auto 2022 abr. 5 arima_auto 2022 may. 6 arima_auto 2022 jun. 7 arima_auto 2022 jul. 8 arima_auto 2022 ago. 9 arima_auto 2022 sep. 10 arima_auto 2022 oct. # ℹ 2 more variables: sales , .mean ``` ::: ::: The best ARIMA MAPE. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} arima_mape <- fabletools::accuracy(beer_arima_fc, beer_2022) %>% select(MAPE) %>% unlist() arima_mape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` MAPE 4.401266 ``` ::: ::: Let's plot the forecast results. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_arima_fc %>% autoplot(beer_tbls, level = NULL) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2022-01-01"), lty = 2) + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![fpp3: ARIMA(4,1,1)(0,1,1)[12] forecast.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-faf-1.png){#fig-faf width=672} ::: ::: The plot above is not very clear. Here is a zoom version. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(beer_zoom, aes(yearmonth(date), sales), lwd = 2, alpha = 0.4) + geom_line() + geom_point(size = 5, color = "black", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "black") + geom_point(aes(y = .mean), size = 5, color = "red", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "red", data = beer_arima_fc) + geom_line(aes(y = .mean), color = "red", size = 0.5, data = beer_arima_fc) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2021-12-01")), linetype = 2) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Sales", caption = c(paste("MAPE=",(round(arima_mape, 5))))) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom. fpp3: ARIMA(4,1,1)(0,1,1)[12] forecast.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-zfaf-1.png){#fig-zfaf width=672} ::: ::: Update the MAPE table. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mape_updated <- mape_updated %>% add_row(.model = "fpp3: ARIMA(4,1,1)(0,1,1)[12]", MAPE = arima_mape) %>% arrange(desc(-MAPE)) mape_updated ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 2 .model MAPE 1 fpp3: ETS(M,A,M) 3.77 2 fpp3: seasonal naïve 3.89 3 fpp3: ARIMA(4,1,1)(0,1,1)[12] 4.40 4 fpp3: naïve 18.1 5 fpp3: drift 20.9 6 fpp3: mean 23.5 ``` ::: ::: ### Neural network. Artificial neural networks are forecasting methods that are based on simple mathematical models of the brain. They allow complex nonlinear relationships between the response variable and its predictors. With time series data, lagged values of the time series can be used as inputs to a neural network, just as we used lagged values in a linear autoregression model. We call this a neural network autoregression or NNAR model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_nnet <- beer_train %>% model(NNETAR(sales)) report(beer_nnet) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Series: sales Model: NNAR(15,1,8)[12] Average of 20 networks, each of which is a 15-8-1 network with 137 weights options were - linear output units sigma^2 estimated as 946.7 ``` ::: ::: The NNAR(15,1,8)[12] model has inputs $y_{t−1}, y_{t−2},..., y_{t−15}$ and 8 neurons in the hidden layer. The forecast for the best NNAR model. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_nnet_fc <- beer_nnet %>% fabletools::forecast(h = "10 months") beer_nnet_fc ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A fable: 10 x 4 [1M] # Key: .model [1] .model date sales .mean 1 NNETAR(sales) 2022 ene. sample[5000] 12882. 2 NNETAR(sales) 2022 feb. sample[5000] 14110. 3 NNETAR(sales) 2022 mar. sample[5000] 16702. 4 NNETAR(sales) 2022 abr. sample[5000] 15968. 5 NNETAR(sales) 2022 may. sample[5000] 16398. 6 NNETAR(sales) 2022 jun. sample[5000] 18597. 7 NNETAR(sales) 2022 jul. sample[5000] 16555. 8 NNETAR(sales) 2022 ago. sample[5000] 17488. 9 NNETAR(sales) 2022 sep. sample[5000] 16552. 10 NNETAR(sales) 2022 oct. sample[5000] 16447. ``` ::: ::: The best NNET MAPE. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} nnet_mape <- fabletools::accuracy(beer_nnet_fc, beer_2022) %>% select(MAPE) %>% unlist() nnet_mape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` MAPE 4.135875 ``` ::: ::: Let's plot the forecast results. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} beer_nnet_fc %>% autoplot(beer_tbls, level = NULL) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.Date("2022-01-01"), lty = 2) + labs(y = "Sales", x = "Date") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![fpp3: NNAR(15,1,8)[12] forecast.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-fnf-1.png){#fig-fnf width=672} ::: ::: The plot above is not very clear. Here is a zoom version. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(beer_zoom, aes(yearmonth(date), sales), lwd = 2, alpha = 0.4) + geom_line() + geom_point(size = 5, color = "black", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "black") + geom_point(aes(y = .mean), size = 5, color = "red", alpha = 0.5, shape = 21, fill = "red", data = beer_nnet_fc) + geom_line(aes(y = .mean), color = "red", size = 0.5, data = beer_nnet_fc) + geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(as.Date("2021-12-01")), linetype = 2) + labs(x = "Date", y = "Sales", caption = c(paste("MAPE=",(round(nnet_mape, 5))))) + theme(legend.position = "bottom") + scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![Zoom. fpp3: NNAR(15,1,8)[12] forecast.](01-forecasting-with-fpp3_files/figure-html/fig-zfnf-1.png){#fig-zfnf width=672} ::: ::: Update the MAPE table. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mape_updated <- mape_updated %>% add_row(.model = "fpp3: NNAR(15,1,8)[12]", MAPE = nnet_mape) %>% arrange(desc(-MAPE)) mape_updated ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 7 × 2 .model MAPE 1 fpp3: ETS(M,A,M) 3.77 2 fpp3: seasonal naïve 3.89 3 fpp3: NNAR(15,1,8)[12] 4.14 4 fpp3: ARIMA(4,1,1)(0,1,1)[12] 4.40 5 fpp3: naïve 18.1 6 fpp3: drift 20.9 7 fpp3: mean 23.5 ``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # I save the mape_updated object to use it in the machine learning chapter. saveRDS(mape_updated, "mape_updated.rds") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # I save the forecast object to use it in the machine learning chapter. fpp3_fc <- bind_rows(beer_sn_fc, beer_ets_fc, beer_arima_fc, beer_nnet_fc) saveRDS(fpp3_fc, "fpp3_fc.rds") ``` :::
